questions.stem: Gupta Traders buys a machine for ₹3,10,000. Expected residual value is ₹10,000 and useful life is 5 years. Under the Straight Line Method, the yearly depreciation is:

options.explanation: This is cost ÷ life. The residual value of ₹10,000 must be deducted first — you never depreciate the amount you expect to recover at the end.
Correct. Depreciable amount = 3,10,000 − 10,000 = 3,00,000; spread over 5 years = ₹60,000 per year.
This ADDS the residual value to cost ((3,10,000 + 10,000) ÷ 5). Residual value is subtracted, not added.
This divides by 6 instead of 5 — a life-counting slip. Life is the number of years of use, not years + 1.

questions.sourceRef: SLM formula: (cost − residual value) ÷ useful life

questions.stem: Sharma & Sons buys a generator: price ₹5,00,000, freight ₹20,000, installation ₹40,000. Expected scrap value is ₹60,000. The depreciable amount is:

options.explanation: This is the total cost but forgets to deduct the scrap value. Depreciable amount = cost − residual value.
Correct. Cost = 5,00,000 + 20,000 + 40,000 = 5,60,000 (freight and installation are capitalised); less scrap 60,000 → ₹5,00,000.
This treats freight and installation as expenses (5,00,000 − 60,000). Costs of bringing the asset to working condition are part of its cost, not revenue expenses.
This deducts the freight (20,000) instead of the scrap value (60,000) — a read-the-numbers slip.

questions.sourceRef: Depreciable amount = cost (incl. bringing-to-use costs) − residual value

questions.stem: An asset costs ₹2,00,000 with residual value ₹20,000 and useful life 9 years. The SLM depreciation rate, expressed as a percentage of original cost, is:

options.explanation: This divides the charge by the depreciable amount (20,000 ÷ 1,80,000). The SLM rate is conventionally expressed on ORIGINAL COST.
This uses a life of 8 years — an off-by-one slip in reading the life.
This divides by cost PLUS residual (20,000 ÷ 2,20,000). Residual value never gets added to cost.

questions.sourceRef: SLM rate % = yearly charge ÷ original cost × 100

questions.stem: A machine costing ₹5,00,000 is depreciated at 10% per annum under the Written Down Value method. Depreciation for the SECOND year is:

options.explanation: This applies the rate to original cost — that is SLM behaviour. Under WDV the rate applies to the opening written-down value each year.
This is the THIRD year's charge (10% × 4,05,000) — one year too far.
This is the average of years 1 and 2 — eyeballing, not computing. Always work year by year.

questions.sourceRef: WDV charge = rate × opening carrying amount

questions.stem: Equipment costing ₹1,00,000 is depreciated at 20% p.a. WDV. Its carrying amount at the end of the THIRD year is:

options.explanation: This removes 20,000 × 3 like SLM. Under WDV each year's charge shrinks because it is computed on the reducing balance.
This is the TOTAL depreciation charged over 3 years (1,00,000 − 51,200) — the question asks for the carrying amount, not the depreciation.
This stops after two years — a counting slip.

questions.sourceRef: WDV closing value = cost × (1 − rate)^n

questions.stem: A machine costs ₹8,00,000 and must be written down to its residual value of ₹1,00,000 in 3 years under WDV. The required rate of depreciation is:

options.explanation: This spreads the depreciable amount straight-line and expresses it on cost ((7,00,000 ÷ 3) ÷ 8,00,000) — an SLM habit; the question demands the WDV rate formula.
This is just residual ÷ cost — the formula stops halfway; you still need the cube root and the subtraction from 1.
This is 1 − 0.125 WITHOUT taking the cube root — the n-th root is the whole point of the formula.

questions.sourceRef: WDV rate = 1 − (residual ÷ cost)^(1/n)

questions.stem: A bottling machine costs ₹3,50,000 (residual value ₹50,000). Its total expected output is 1,50,000 crates over an expected 5-year life. This year it produced 24,000 crates. Depreciation under the units-of-production method is:

options.explanation: This applies the output fraction to COST (3,50,000 × 24/150) instead of to the depreciable amount — the residual value must come out first.
Correct. Depreciable amount 3,00,000 × (24,000 ÷ 1,50,000) = ₹48,000.
This is time-based SLM (3,00,000 ÷ 5). The question specifies the units method — the 5-year life is a distractor.
This is SLM on cost (3,50,000 ÷ 5) — two slips at once: wrong method AND residual ignored.

questions.sourceRef: Units method: depreciable amount × year's units ÷ lifetime units

questions.stem: Patel Furniture (books closed every 31 March) buys a machine for ₹2,50,000 on 1 July 2025. Depreciation is 12% p.a. SLM on cost. Depreciation for the year 2025–26 is:

options.explanation: This charges a full year. The machine was owned only from 1 July — charge only 9 months (July to March).
This charges only 6 months — a month-counting slip (July to March is 9 months, not 6).
This charges 10 months — the count wrongly includes April 2026.

questions.sourceRef: Pro-rata: charge from date ready for use to year end

questions.stem: Machinery bought on 1 April 2022 for ₹4,00,000 is depreciated at 10% p.a. SLM on cost (year end 31 March). On 30 September 2024 it is sold for ₹3,20,000. The result of the sale is:

options.text: Profit ₹20,000

options.explanation: This compares the sale price with original COST (4,00,000 − 3,20,000). Profit or loss on sale is always measured against WDV.

options.text: Profit ₹40,000
options.explanation: This charges a FULL year for 2024–25 (WDV 2,80,000). Depreciation stops on the date of sale — only 6 months accrue.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: This forgets the final 6 months entirely (WDV taken as 3,20,000 at the last year-end). Depreciation must be charged up to 30 September.

questions.sourceRef: Profit/loss on sale = sale price − WDV at date of sale

questions.stem: A machine costing ₹6,40,000 is depreciated at 25% p.a. WDV. Exactly two full years later, on the first day of year 3, it is sold for ₹4,00,000. The result is:

options.text: Profit ₹40,000

options.explanation: This compares sale price with original cost. An asset sold above WDV gives a PROFIT even though it sold below cost.

options.text: Profit ₹80,000
options.explanation: This computes both years' depreciation on COST (1,60,000 × 2, WDV 3,20,000) — SLM behaviour applied to a WDV question.
The magnitude is right but the sign is flipped — sale price (4,00,000) EXCEEDS WDV (3,60,000), so it is a profit.

questions.sourceRef: Profit/loss on sale = sale price − WDV; WDV = cost × (1−rate)^n

questions.stem: Plant costing ₹9,00,000 (residual ₹60,000, life 7 years, SLM) has been depreciated for 4 years. At the start of year 5 the TOTAL life is revised to 6 years and the residual value to ₹20,000. Depreciation for year 5 is:

options.explanation: This is the original charge ((9,00,000 − 60,000) ÷ 7) — it ignores the revision entirely.
Correct. Carrying amount after 4 years = 9,00,000 − 4 × 1,20,000 = 4,20,000. Remaining life = 6 − 4 = 2 years. Charge = (4,20,000 − 20,000) ÷ 2 = ₹2,00,000.
This spreads the full carrying amount (4,20,000 ÷ 2) and forgets the revised residual value of ₹20,000.
This uses the OLD residual value ((4,20,000 − 60,000) ÷ 2). The question revises the residual too — read both revisions.

questions.sourceRef: AS 10 change in estimate — prospective: (carrying amount − revised residual) ÷ remaining life

questions.stem: A machine costing ₹5,00,000 was depreciated at 20% WDV for 2 years. From year 3 the firm switches to SLM; remaining life is 4 years and residual value ₹40,000. Depreciation for year 3 is:

options.explanation: Correct. WDV after 2 years: 5,00,000 → 4,00,000 → 3,20,000. SLM prospectively: (3,20,000 − 40,000) ÷ 4 = ₹70,000. Under AS 10 a change of method is a change in ESTIMATE — applied prospectively.
This spreads 3,20,000 ÷ 4 and forgets the residual value of ₹40,000.
This recomputes SLM retrospectively from day one ((5,00,000 − 40,000) ÷ 6). The old retrospective treatment belongs to the pre-revision era — current treatment is prospective.
This continues WDV (20% × 3,20,000) — it ignores the change of method.

questions.sourceRef: AS 10 — change of depreciation method treated as change in estimate (prospective)

questions.stem: An asset costing ₹6,00,000 (nil residual, life 10 years, SLM) has run for 6 years. At the start of year 7, management decides the asset will last only 2 MORE years. Depreciation for year 7 is:

options.explanation: The original charge — ignores the revision.
Correct. Carrying amount = 6,00,000 − 6 × 60,000 = 2,40,000; spread over the 2 remaining years = ₹1,20,000.
This spreads over 3 years — misreading '2 more years' as 'total life 9'. The remaining life is exactly 2.
This divides the carrying amount by the revised TOTAL life of 8 (2,40,000 ÷ 8) — the divisor is the REMAINING life, because past years are already charged.

questions.sourceRef: AS 10 change in estimate — carrying amount ÷ remaining revised life

questions.stem: An asset costs ₹2,40,000 (residual ₹40,000, life 10 years, SLM). The firm uses a Provision for Depreciation account. After 4 full years, the balance in the Provision for Depreciation account is:

options.explanation: Correct. Yearly charge = (2,40,000 − 40,000) ÷ 10 = 20,000; accumulated over 4 years = ₹80,000. The asset account itself still shows ₹2,40,000.
This accumulates cost ÷ 10 (24,000 × 4) — the residual value was ignored in the yearly charge.
This is the asset's CARRYING AMOUNT (2,40,000 − 80,000), not the provision balance. The provision holds the depreciation, not the remaining value.
Three years accumulated instead of four — a counting slip.

questions.sourceRef: Provision for Depreciation = accumulated yearly charges; asset stays at cost

questions.stem: A machine costs ₹4,20,000 (residual ₹20,000). Total expected working life is 20,000 machine-hours over roughly 10 years. This year it ran 2,500 hours. Depreciation under the machine-hour method is:

options.explanation: This applies the hours fraction to COST (4,20,000 × 2,500/20,000) — deduct the residual value first.
This is time-based SLM on the depreciable amount (4,00,000 ÷ 10) — the method asked for is usage-based; the 10-year figure is a distractor.
This is SLM on cost (4,20,000 ÷ 10) — wrong method AND residual ignored.

questions.sourceRef: Machine-hour method: depreciable amount × year's hours ÷ lifetime hours

questions.stem: A machine costing ₹2,00,000 is depreciated at 10% p.a. WDV. Depreciation for the THIRD year is:

options.explanation: 10% of cost — that is the year-1 charge (and the SLM habit). By year 3 the base has shrunk twice.
That is the SECOND year's charge — stopped one year early.
That is the FOURTH year's charge — one multiplication too many.

questions.sourceRef: WDV year-n charge = rate × cost × (1−rate)^(n−1)

questions.stem: A software licence is bought for ₹1,50,000 with a legal life of 10 years and no residual value. The yearly amortisation (straight line) is:

options.explanation: Correct. 1,50,000 ÷ 10 = ₹15,000 per year. Amortisation is the same spreading idea applied to an intangible asset.

options.text: Nil — intangible assets are not written off
options.explanation: Intangibles with finite lives ARE amortised (AS 26). Only assets with unlimited life (like land) escape the charge.
This assumes a 5-year life. Use the life given in the question — here the legal life is 10 years.
This expenses the whole licence in year 1 — that defeats the matching idea; the licence serves the business for 10 years.

questions.sourceRef: AS 26 — amortisation of finite-life intangibles

questions.stem: Mining rights cost ₹50,00,000 for a mine holding an estimated 2,00,000 tonnes of ore. This year 15,000 tonnes were extracted. The depletion charge for the year is:

options.explanation: Correct. 50,00,000 × 15,000 ÷ 2,00,000 = ₹3,75,000 — depletion follows extraction, exactly like the units-of-production idea.
This charges a flat 5% per year (a time-based habit). Depletion follows OUTPUT, not the calendar.
This uses 30,000 tonnes — double the actual extraction; a numbers-reading slip.
This charges 10% per year — a WDV-rate habit transferred to a depletion question.

questions.sourceRef: Depletion = cost × units extracted ÷ total estimated units

questions.stem: Under the Provision for Depreciation method: an asset with original cost ₹3,00,000 and accumulated depreciation ₹1,10,000 is sold for ₹1,75,000. The result on disposal is:

options.explanation: This compares sale price with original cost (3,00,000 − 1,75,000) — the accumulated depreciation must be netted off first.

options.text: Profit ₹65,000
options.explanation: This compares the sale price with the PROVISION balance (1,75,000 − 1,10,000) — a meaningless comparison; compare with WDV.

options.text: Profit ₹15,000
options.explanation: Right magnitude, wrong sign — the sale price is BELOW the WDV of 1,90,000, so it is a loss.

questions.sourceRef: Disposal: WDV = cost − accumulated provision; result = sale − WDV

questions.stem: A van costs ₹5,00,000 with residual value ₹50,000 and life 6 years (SLM). The TOTAL depreciation charged over the van's whole life is:

options.explanation: Correct. Total depreciation over the full life always equals the depreciable amount: 5,00,000 − 50,000 = ₹4,50,000. SLM lands exactly on residual value.
The full cost is never depreciated when a residual value exists — ₹50,000 comes back on disposal.
That is ONE year's charge (4,50,000 ÷ 6), not the lifetime total.
This adds the residual to cost — depreciation can never exceed what was spent.

questions.sourceRef: Lifetime depreciation = depreciable amount

questions.stem: A firm charges SLM depreciation of ₹36,000 per year on a machine with useful life 8 years and residual value ₹12,000. The original cost of the machine is:

options.explanation: Correct. Depreciable amount = 36,000 × 8 = 2,88,000; add back the residual: 2,88,000 + 12,000 = ₹3,00,000.
This multiplies back the charge (36,000 × 8) but forgets to ADD the residual value — that figure is the depreciable amount, not the cost.
This SUBTRACTS the residual instead of adding it (2,88,000 − 12,000) — direction confusion when working backwards.
This uses a 10-year life (36,000 × 10 + 12,000) — a habit figure; the life given is 8.

questions.sourceRef: Cost = SLM charge × life + residual value

questions.stem: Office equipment costing ₹2,50,000 is depreciated at 20% p.a. WDV. Its book value at the end of the SECOND year is:

options.explanation: This removes 50,000 twice — SLM behaviour. The year-2 charge is 20% of 2,00,000 (i.e. 40,000), not 50,000.
That is the value after ONE year — a counting slip.
That is the value after THREE years — one year too far.

questions.sourceRef: WDV book value = cost × (1−rate)^n

questions.stem: A machine bought on 1 April 2023 for ₹1,80,000 is depreciated at 10% p.a. SLM on cost (year end 31 March). It is sold on 31 December 2025 for ₹1,25,000. The result of the sale is:

options.text: Loss ₹5,500

options.explanation: This compares the sale price with original cost (1,80,000 − 1,25,000) — always compare with WDV.

options.text: Loss ₹1,000
options.explanation: This charges a FULL year for 2025–26 (WDV 1,26,000). The machine left on 31 December — only 9 months accrue.

options.text: Profit ₹5,500
options.explanation: Right magnitude, flipped sign — the sale price is below WDV, so it is a loss.

questions.sourceRef: Pro-rata to disposal date; result = sale − WDV

questions.stem: Verma Foods buys a packing machine: price ₹3,00,000, installation ₹20,000, freight inward ₹16,000, and a separate ANNUAL maintenance contract of ₹20,000. Depreciation for year 1 at 25% WDV is:

options.explanation: This depreciates the bare price only — installation and freight are part of the cost of bringing the machine to working condition.
This capitalises installation but expenses the freight — freight inward on an asset purchase is also capitalised.
Correct. Cost = 3,00,000 + 20,000 + 16,000 = 3,36,000; year 1 at 25% = ₹84,000. The maintenance contract is a yearly REVENUE expense, never capitalised.
This wrongly capitalises the annual maintenance contract too ((3,36,000 + 20,000) × 25%). Recurring running costs are revenue expenditure.

questions.sourceRef: Cost = price + bringing-to-use costs; revenue expenses excluded

questions.stem: Depreciation accounting is best described as a process of:

options.text: Valuation of the asset at market price each year
options.explanation: Depreciation does NOT track market value — a van's book value can differ wildly from its resale price.

options.text: Allocation of the depreciable amount over the asset's useful life
options.explanation: Correct. Depreciation spreads (allocates) cost over the years of use — allocation, not valuation, is the standard one-word answer.

options.text: Setting aside cash to replace the asset
options.explanation: Depreciation involves no cash movement at all — the cash left when the asset was bought. Replacement funding is a separate decision.

options.text: Recording repairs and maintenance systematically
options.explanation: Repairs are revenue expenses recorded when incurred — completely separate from depreciation.

questions.sourceRef: Depreciation = systematic allocation of depreciable amount over useful life

questions.stem: A GST-billing machine at Iyer Stores works perfectly, but a new UPI-linked model makes it uneconomic to keep using. The machine's loss of usefulness is caused by:

options.text: Wear and tear
options.explanation: The machine works perfectly — nothing has physically worn out.

options.text: Obsolescence
options.explanation: Correct. A newer technology has made the old asset uneconomic — that is obsolescence, and it hits working assets too.

options.text: Depletion
options.explanation: Depletion applies to natural resources being physically used up (mines, quarries) — not to machines.

options.text: Passage of time
options.explanation: Time-based loss applies to rights that expire (like a lease) — here the cause is the NEW technology, not the calendar.

questions.sourceRef: Causes of depreciation: wear & tear, time, obsolescence

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT depreciated or amortised?

options.text: Freehold land
options.explanation: Correct. Freehold land has an unlimited useful life, so there is nothing to spread. (A leasehold, by contrast, IS amortised over the lease term.)

options.text: A building
options.explanation: Buildings wear out and are depreciated — only the land under them is not.

options.text: A patent
options.explanation: Patents have finite legal lives and are amortised (AS 26).

options.text: A delivery van kept idle all year
options.explanation: Idle assets still lose value through time and obsolescence — depreciation continues.

questions.sourceRef: Land: unlimited life → no depreciation

questions.stem: Mehta & Co. made a heavy loss this year, and its machinery stood idle for four months. Regarding depreciation for the year, the firm must:

options.text: Skip it — there is no profit to charge it against
options.explanation: Depreciation is not a profit-sharing item; it is a cost of using (and holding) the asset, charged in loss years too.

options.text: Charge it in full as usual
options.explanation: Correct. Depreciation is charged regardless of profit and regardless of idleness — value is lost through time and obsolescence even when the machine rests.

options.text: Charge only 8 months since the machinery was idle for 4
options.explanation: Idle months are not exempt — pro-rata applies to OWNERSHIP period (purchase/sale dates), not to usage gaps.

options.text: Defer it and charge double next year
options.explanation: Deferring understates this year's loss and breaks the matching idea. Each year bears its own charge.

questions.sourceRef: Depreciation is mandatory — independent of profit and usage

questions.stem: Depreciation is called a 'non-cash expense' because:

options.text: No cash leaves the business when depreciation is recorded
options.explanation: Correct. The cash went out when the asset was purchased; the yearly depreciation entry only allocates that old cost — no fresh cash moves.

options.text: It is not a real expense
options.explanation: It IS a real expense — the asset genuinely loses usefulness. 'Non-cash' describes the timing of cash, not the reality of the cost.

options.text: It is paid through the bank, never in cash
options.explanation: It is not 'paid' at all — there is no payee.

options.text: It appears only in the cash flow statement
options.explanation: Backwards — depreciation appears in the P&L, and is ADDED BACK in a cash flow statement precisely because it involved no cash.

questions.sourceRef: Non-cash charge — cash outflow occurred at purchase

questions.stem: Match the write-off to the asset: a coal mine, a patent, and a lathe machine are written off respectively through:

options.text: Depletion, amortisation, depreciation
options.explanation: Correct. Natural resources are DEPLETED as they are extracted; intangibles are AMORTISED; tangible machines are DEPRECIATED. Same idea, three names.

options.text: Depreciation, depletion, amortisation
options.explanation: This rotates the labels one place — a mine is not 'depreciated'; its ore is physically used up (depletion).

options.text: Amortisation, depreciation, depletion
options.explanation: Rotated the other way — a patent is intangible, so its write-off is amortisation, not depreciation.

options.text: Depletion, depreciation, amortisation
options.explanation: The mine is right, but the patent (intangible → amortisation) and lathe (tangible → depreciation) are swapped.

questions.sourceRef: Depreciation / amortisation / depletion by asset class

questions.stem: Which statement about the two main methods is CORRECT?

options.text: Under SLM the carrying amount reaches exactly the residual value at the end of the useful life
options.explanation: Correct. SLM removes the same slice of the depreciable amount every year, so after the last year the book value equals the residual value exactly — a handy self-check.

options.text: Under WDV the carrying amount reaches zero at the end of the useful life
options.explanation: WDV multiplies by (1 − rate) each year and mathematically never reaches zero — it only approaches the residual value.

options.text: Under SLM the yearly charge falls every year
options.explanation: The falling charge is WDV's signature; SLM's charge is constant.

options.text: Under WDV the charge is the same every year
options.explanation: Swapped definitions — the constant charge is SLM.

questions.sourceRef: SLM ends at residual; WDV never reaches zero

questions.stem: WDV is often preferred for vehicles and machinery because:

options.text: High early depreciation plus rising later repair costs keeps the total yearly burden roughly even
options.explanation: Correct. New machines need few repairs (high depreciation year); old machines need many repairs (low depreciation year) — WDV balances the two.

options.text: It charges less depreciation in total over the asset's life
options.explanation: Total lifetime depreciation is the depreciable amount under EVERY method — only the yearly pattern differs.

options.text: It is the only method the law permits for machinery
options.explanation: No such rule — the method should match the pattern in which the asset's benefits are consumed.

options.text: It avoids the need to estimate useful life
options.explanation: Life estimation is still needed (to set the rate, and for disclosures) — WDV does not remove estimates.

questions.sourceRef: Method suitability: depreciation + repairs ≈ even total burden

questions.stem: Under AS 10, a change in the METHOD of depreciation (e.g., WDV to SLM) is accounted for as:

options.text: A change in accounting estimate — applied prospectively
options.explanation: Correct. AS 10 treats the depreciation method as an estimate of how the asset's benefits are consumed; a change applies from now on, with no reopening of past years.

options.text: A change in accounting policy — recomputed retrospectively from the asset's purchase
options.explanation: This was the OLD treatment under the pre-revision standard (AS 6 era) — older books still show it; the current answer is prospective.

options.text: A prior-period error, corrected through last year's figures
options.explanation: Nothing was WRONG earlier — an estimate changed. Errors and estimate-changes are different categories.

options.text: Not permitted once a method is chosen
options.explanation: A change is permitted when the new method better reflects the consumption pattern — it is the accounting treatment, not the permission, that is tested.

questions.sourceRef: AS 10 — method change = change in estimate (prospective)

questions.stem: Three years into an asset's 10-year estimated life, management revises the total life to 7 years. The correct treatment is:

options.text: Spread the current carrying amount (less residual) over the remaining 4 years
options.explanation: Correct. Prospective treatment: take the book value as it stands and spread it over the REMAINING revised life (7 − 3 = 4 years).

options.text: Recompute all three past years at the 7-year life and pass a catch-up entry
options.explanation: That is retrospective treatment — estimate changes never reopen past years.

options.text: Continue the old charge and write off the shortfall in the final year
options.explanation: This dumps the correction into one distorted final year — exactly what prospective spreading avoids.

options.text: Restate last year's financial statements
options.explanation: Restatement is for errors. A genuine change in estimate is new information, not a mistake.

questions.sourceRef: AS 10 — life revision applied prospectively over remaining life

questions.stem: Profit or loss on the sale of a depreciable asset is measured as the difference between the sale price and:

options.text: The written-down value on the date of sale
options.explanation: Correct. The books carry the asset at WDV, so WDV is the benchmark. Selling below cost but above WDV still yields a profit.

options.text: The original cost
options.explanation: Cost ignores the depreciation already charged — using it double-counts the wear already expensed.

options.text: The current market price of a similar new asset
options.explanation: Market prices of new assets are irrelevant to the books of the old one.

options.text: The residual value estimated at purchase
options.explanation: The residual estimate was only an input for computing the charge — the benchmark on sale is the actual WDV.

questions.sourceRef: Result on sale = sale price − WDV

questions.stem: When a firm uses a Provision for Depreciation account, the Machinery account in the ledger shows:

options.text: The original cost of the machinery
options.explanation: Correct. The whole point of the provision method: the asset account stays at cost, and accumulated depreciation collects separately in the provision account.

options.text: The written-down value
options.explanation: WDV appears under the DIRECT method (asset credited each year). With a provision account, WDV is only a net figure (cost − provision) shown in the balance sheet.

options.text: Cost minus residual value
options.explanation: The depreciable amount is a computation input — no ledger account shows it as a balance.

options.text: The current market value
options.explanation: Market value never enters the historical-cost ledger.

questions.sourceRef: Provision method: asset account stays at original cost

questions.stem: Under the DIRECT method (no provision account), the yearly depreciation entry is:

options.text: Depreciation A/c Dr — To Asset A/c
options.explanation: Correct. The expense account is debited and the asset itself is credited, so the asset's balance falls to WDV each year. (Depreciation A/c is then closed to P&L.)

options.text: Asset A/c Dr — To Depreciation A/c
options.explanation: Reversed — this would INCREASE the asset every year.

options.text: Depreciation A/c Dr — To Provision for Depreciation A/c
options.explanation: That is the PROVISION method's entry — the question says no provision account is kept.

options.text: P&L A/c Dr — To Cash A/c
options.explanation: Depreciation involves no cash — there is never a Cash A/c credit in a depreciation entry.

questions.sourceRef: Direct method journal entry

questions.stem: 'Depreciable amount' means:

options.text: Cost of the asset minus its residual value
options.explanation: Correct. This is the amount actually spread over the useful life — the recoverable residual is never depreciated.

options.text: The original cost of the asset
options.explanation: Cost is the starting point, but the depreciable amount removes the expected recovery at the end.

options.text: Cost minus depreciation charged to date
options.explanation: That is the CARRYING AMOUNT (written-down value) — a different term the exam loves to swap in.

options.text: The yearly depreciation charge
options.explanation: The charge is one year's slice OF the depreciable amount, not the amount itself.

questions.sourceRef: Depreciable amount = cost − residual value

questions.stem: The 'useful life' of an asset, for depreciation purposes, is:

options.text: The period over which the BUSINESS expects to use it (or the output it expects from it)
options.explanation: Correct. Useful life is an entity-specific estimate — it can be expressed in years OR in units/hours of output, and it is usually shorter than the asset's total physical life.

options.text: The total physical life of the asset until it stops working
options.explanation: A firm may plan to replace vans every 5 years though they run for 12 — the USE period governs, not the physical maximum.

options.text: Exactly the period in the manufacturer's warranty
options.explanation: Warranties are commercial promises, not accounting estimates.

options.text: A fixed statutory number that cannot be changed once set
options.explanation: It is an estimate, revisited when facts change — revisions are applied prospectively.

questions.sourceRef: Useful life = expected period/output of use by the entity

questions.stem: Other things equal, a HIGHER estimated residual value results in:

options.text: Lower yearly depreciation
options.explanation: Correct. A higher residual shrinks the depreciable amount (cost − residual), so each year's slice is smaller.

options.text: Higher yearly depreciation
options.explanation: Direction reversed — more expected recovery at the end means LESS to spread.

options.text: No effect on the yearly charge
options.explanation: Residual value sits inside the SLM numerator — it always affects the charge (except in the WDV charge formula, where it sets the rate instead).

options.text: A shorter useful life
options.explanation: Residual value and useful life are independent estimates — one does not move the other.

questions.sourceRef: Charge varies inversely with residual value (SLM)

questions.stem: A machine was delivered on 1 May, installed and ready for use on 1 June, but actual production started on 1 August. Depreciation begins from:

options.text: 1 June — when the asset was ready for use
options.explanation: Correct. Depreciation runs from the date the asset is AVAILABLE for use — waiting for production to start does not postpone it.

options.text: 1 August — when production began
options.explanation: Actual usage is not the trigger; availability is. The idle June–July period still consumes the asset's life.

options.text: 1 May — the delivery date
options.explanation: On 1 May the machine was not yet in working condition — cost accumulation (installation) was still ongoing.

options.text: The start of the next financial year
options.explanation: There is no 'wait for a fresh year' rule — pro-rata handles mid-year starts.

questions.sourceRef: Depreciation from date available/ready for use (AS 10 concept)

questions.stem: Charging depreciation every year, instead of expensing the whole asset in the purchase year, follows which accounting idea?

options.text: Matching — cost should sit against the income it helps earn
options.explanation: Correct. The asset earns income for many years, so its cost is matched against those years, not dumped on year one.

options.text: Conservatism — anticipate all possible losses
options.explanation: Conservatism is about caution with uncertain gains/losses; depreciation is systematic allocation, not caution.

options.text: Money measurement — record only what money can measure
options.explanation: That concept says WHAT gets recorded, not WHEN costs are recognised.

options.text: Dual aspect — every debit has a credit
options.explanation: Dual aspect is true of every entry, so it explains nothing specific about depreciation timing.

questions.sourceRef: Matching concept — allocation rationale

questions.stem: A firm using WDV notices the asset's book value never becomes zero. This is because:

options.text: Each year's charge is a percentage of a shrinking balance, so a remainder always survives
options.explanation: Correct. Multiplying by (1 − rate) repeatedly approaches, but never reaches, zero — mathematically the book value only tends toward the residual value.

options.text: The firm has been calculating wrongly
options.explanation: Nothing is wrong — never-reaching-zero is an inherent property of the reducing-balance method.

options.text: WDV is not allowed to run for the full useful life
options.explanation: No such restriction exists.

options.text: Residual value keeps being added back each year
options.explanation: Nothing is added back — the balance simply shrinks by a proportion, not by a fixed amount.

questions.sourceRef: Reducing balance: value = cost × (1−r)^n > 0 for all n

questions.stem: Which of these amounts spent on a newly purchased second-hand lorry is NOT added to its cost for depreciation purposes?

options.text: Overhauling the engine before first use
options.explanation: Pre-use overhaul brings the lorry to working condition — capitalised.

options.text: Fresh registration and number-plate transfer fees
options.explanation: Necessary to put the lorry on the road for the first time — capitalised.

options.text: Diesel and driver's salary for the first month of running
options.explanation: Correct — these are running (revenue) costs of USING the lorry, not of acquiring it. They go straight to the P&L.

options.text: Freight for bringing the lorry from the seller's city
options.explanation: Bringing the asset to its location is part of cost — capitalised.

questions.sourceRef: Capitalise bringing-to-use costs; expense running costs

questions.stem: For companies registered under the Companies Act 2013, indicative useful lives of assets for computing depreciation are given in:

options.text: Schedule II of the Companies Act 2013
options.explanation: Correct. Schedule II ('Useful Lives to Compute Depreciation') gives the useful-life framework companies follow — Foundation needs only this mapping.

options.text: Schedule III of the Companies Act 2013
options.explanation: Schedule III prescribes the FORMAT of financial statements, not useful lives.

options.text: AS 2
options.explanation: AS 2 deals with inventory valuation — unrelated to depreciation.

options.text: The GST rate schedules
options.explanation: GST schedules classify goods/services for tax rates — nothing to do with depreciation.

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, Schedule II

questions.stem: Define depreciation. State its three main causes and explain why depreciation must be charged even in a year of loss. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Definition: systematic allocation of the depreciable amount (cost − residual value) of an asset over its useful life; allocation, not valuation
skeleton.citation: AS 10 concept; Companies Act 2013 Sch II Part A defines the same idea

skeleton.point: Cause 1 — wear and tear from use
Cause 2 — passage of time / expiry of legal rights
Cause 3 — obsolescence (new technology makes the asset uneconomic)
Charged in loss years: it is a cost of using the asset, needed for true profit/loss (matching); not dependent on profits or usage

questions.sourceRef: Depreciation definition, causes, mandatory nature

questions.stem: Distinguish between the Straight Line Method and the Written Down Value method of depreciation (any four points), and state one type of asset each method suits. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Base: SLM — on original cost (constant); WDV — on opening book value (reducing)
Yearly charge: SLM equal every year; WDV highest in year 1, falls each year
End value: SLM reaches residual value exactly; WDV never reaches zero
Total burden with repairs: WDV + rising repairs ≈ even total; SLM total burden rises as repairs grow
Suitability: SLM — assets with even service (furniture, buildings, leases); WDV — vehicles/machinery with heavy early benefit

questions.sourceRef: SLM vs WDV distinctions

questions.stem: On 1 April 2023 Rathi Mills bought machinery for ₹6,00,000 (SLM, 10% p.a. on cost, year end 31 March). On 1 October 2025, one-third of the machinery (original cost ₹2,00,000) was sold for ₹1,42,000. Prepare the Machinery Account for 2023–24 to 2025–26. (10 marks)

skeleton.point: 2023–24: full-year depreciation 60,000 (10% × 6,00,000); closing balance 5,40,000
2024–25: depreciation 60,000; closing balance 4,80,000
2025–26 on sold part: 6 months' depreciation = 10,000 (10% × 2,00,000 × 6/12)

2025–26 on retained part (cost 4,00,000): full-year depreciation 40,000; closing balance = 4,00,000 − (3 × 40,000) = 2,80,000, shown as balancing figure with dates and particulars in proper account format

questions.sourceRef: Machinery account with part-sale; pro-rata; loss on sale

questions.stem: What is the accounting treatment when (a) the useful life of an asset is revised, and (b) the method of depreciation is changed? Illustrate (a) with a simple example. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: (a) Change in estimate → prospective: (carrying amount − revised residual) ÷ remaining revised life; past years untouched
Example: cost 1,00,000, life 5, SLM 20,000/yr; after 2 years carrying 60,000; life revised to total 4 → charge 60,000 ÷ 2 = 30,000
(b) Method change is ALSO a change in estimate under AS 10 → prospective from the carrying amount; no retrospective recomputation
skeleton.citation: AS 10 — method = estimate of consumption pattern

questions.sourceRef: AS 10 revisions — prospective treatment

questions.stem: Explain the Provision for Depreciation method. Pass the journal entries for (i) the yearly depreciation charge and (ii) transfer of the relevant balances when the asset is sold. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Concept: asset account remains at original cost; yearly charges accumulate in a separate Provision for Depreciation (accumulated depreciation) account
(i) Depreciation A/c Dr → To Provision for Depreciation A/c; then P&L A/c Dr → To Depreciation A/c
(ii) On sale: Asset Disposal A/c Dr → To Asset A/c (original cost); Provision for Depreciation A/c Dr → To Asset Disposal A/c (accumulated amount)
Bank A/c Dr → To Asset Disposal A/c (sale proceeds); balance of Disposal A/c = profit (Cr) or loss (Dr) → transferred to P&L

questions.sourceRef: Provision method mechanics + disposal entries

questions.stem: When is the units-of-production (or machine-hour) method more appropriate than time-based methods? Give the formula and one business example. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Appropriate when wear depends on USAGE, not time — output/hours drive the loss of usefulness
Formula: (cost − residual value) × units (or hours) this year ÷ total expected lifetime units (or hours)
Example: a bottling machine rated for a fixed number of crates; a stone-crusher rated in machine-hours; charge follows each year's actual output

questions.sourceRef: Units-of-production method
